subject: Physics
title: Tokamaks and Plasma
description: A 60-second lesson on Tokamaks and Plasma.

hook.text: To fuse atoms, you need to heat gas to 100 million degrees—hotter than the Sun's core.

buildup.text: At these temperatures, atoms strip apart into plasma—a soup of free electrons and nuclei.

discovery.text: Tokamaks use powerful magnets shaped like a doughnut to contain this plasma without it touching any wall.

twist.text: The plasma is so hot that any contact with the wall would melt the reactor instantly.

climax.text: The Soviet Union invented tokamaks in the 1950s. They remain the leading approach to fusion today.

punchline.text: A magnetic doughnut holding star-fire. That's a tokamak.

quiz.question: How does a tokamak contain plasma?
options: Using powerful magnets in a doughnut shape
By cooling the plasma with liquid nitrogen
Using laser beams to compress it
